title: Rollback may not undo data
description: Reverting code can leave migrated data, emitted events, external API calls, cache state, or user-visible side effects behind.
body: Use when evaluating rollback or release safety. Do not claim reversibility without state evidence. Actions: identify non-code state touched by the change and whether it has a restoration, compensation, or disablement path. Validation signals include dry-run, backup, compatibility checks, and post-rollback smoke. Risk boundary: irreversible state change needs human approval. Use when Production Change Risk Controls work touches repository conventions, toolchain files, changed symbols, validation commands, and runtime output. Do not use this guidance to override a more specific pack, user instruction, or repository contract. Actions: use the fact to constrain the change and test plan; inspect the current files first, keep the change scoped to the owning boundary, and record what evidence changed the decision. Validation signals include package-local checks, focused tests, build output, smoke runs, and command logs. Risk boundary: escalate or stop when the task involves broad rewrites, generated-file drift, stale assumptions, and unvalidated behavior changes, missing owner evidence, or live external state.
